questions.question: A resultant force of $16\text{ N}$ acts for $5\text{ s}$ on an object of mass $2\text{ kg}$. What is the change in the object's speed?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Impulse $= F \times t = 16 \times 5 = 80\text{ N s}$. Step 2: Change in momentum $Delta p = Impulse = m Delta v$. So $80 = 2 \times Delta v$, which means $Delta v = 40\text{ m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Providing the impulse ($80$) as the speed change.

questions.question: A basketball hits the ground 10 \times in $20\text{ seconds}$. Each collision results in a change in momentum of $15\text{ kg m/s}$. What is the average force exerted by the ground on the ball?
questions.explanation:
Total change in momentum $Delta p = 10 \times 15 = 150\text{ kg m/s}$. Total time $t = 20\text{ s}$. Average Force $= Delta p / t = 150 / 20 = 7.5\text{ N}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using only a single collision's momentum change ($15/20 = 0.75\text{ N}$).

questions.question: A $0.4\text{ kg}$ ball travels at $5\text{ m/s}$, hits a wall, and rebounds in the opposite direction at $5\text{ m/s}$. Calculate the magnitude of the impulse given to the ball.
questions.explanation:
Momentum is a vector. Taking initial direction as positive ($+5$), final velocity is $-5$. Change in momentum $= m(v - u) = 0.4 \times (-5 - 5) = -4.0\text{ kg m/s}$. Magnitude is $4.0$. 

**Common mistake:** Subtracting magnitudes to get zero or ignoring the rebound to get $2.0$.

questions.question: A $2000\text{ kg}$ empty freight car moving at $8\text{ m/s}$ collides and sticks to a stationary $8000\text{ kg}$ loaded car. What is the impulse exerted by the loaded car on the empty car during the collision?
questions.explanation:
Using conservation of momentum: $(2000 \times 8) + 0 = (2000 + 8000) \times V implies 16000 = 10000V implies V = 1.6\text{ m/s}$. Impulse on empty car $= Delta p = m(v - u) = 2000(1.6 - 8) = -12800\text{ N s}$. Magnitude is $12800$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the total initial momentum ($16000$) as the impulse on a single car.

questions.question: Under what condition is the equation $F = ma$ exactly the same as $F = \frac{Delta p}{Delta t}$?
options: When the mass of the object does not change
Only when the object starts from rest
When the velocity of the object is constant
questions.explanation:
The note states $F = Delta p / Delta t$ is the same as $F = ma$ for special cases when the mass of the object does not change. If mass changes, the derivative of $mv$ includes a mass change term. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking they are always identical under all physics conditions.
